ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh; closest defensible anchor is 'lamb dish' (FOODON_03302757). A more specific Awadhi slow-cooked lamb shank term is absent from the ontology as of 2024.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Awadhi dum-cooked lamb shank. A00GJ (meat-based dish, lamb/mutton, cooked, with sauce/gravy) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Awadhi lamb shank preparation. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual regional curry dishes at this level of specificity.

preparation_method: Dum cooking (slow cooking in a sealed pot)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh is approximately 400 g, comprising one full lamb shank with bone and a generous portion of qorma gravy, as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mutton/Lamb Curry (cooked, with gravy) and USDA FoodData Central — Lamb Shank, cooked, braised (NDB 17082)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Mutton curry with gravy' (cooked) as the primary regional anchor, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 17082 (lamb shank, cooked, braised) for protein, fat, and saturated fat. Ghee and cream additions are reflected in the elevated fat and saturated fat values. The input record's per_100g values (energy 187.5 kcal, protein 13.75 g, fat 12.5 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. All values are marked medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — Meat-based curry with low-carbohydrate gravy analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with low carbohydrate content (5 g/100g) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 20 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.0 (low GL). The dominant macronutrient is protein and fat; carbohydrate contribution is minimal, supporting a low GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.25 g/100g from lamb, ghee, and cream contributes to arterial stiffness; sodium 225 mg/100g is within acceptable range but cumulative intake across a 400 g serving reaches 900 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request preparation with reduced ghee and cream, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to offset sodium load; the saturated fat content is the primary concern rather than sodium alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is very low at 5.0 g/100g with sugar at 1.25 g/100g; high protein at 13.75 g/100g from lamb shank slows gastric emptying and attenuates post-meal glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (200–300 g); pair with a high-fibre side dish and monitor for the insulin-resistance effect of frequent high saturated fat intake from ghee and cream.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.25 g/100g and trans fat at 0.25 g/100g from lamb, ghee, and cream elevate LDL cholesterol; cholesterol content is 50 mg/100g; a 400 g serving delivers 25 g saturated fat, substantially exceeding the daily limit of 20 g for CVD patients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence, limit serving size to 100–150 g, request preparation with minimal ghee and no cream, and avoid pairing with other high-saturated-fat foods on the same day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies joint inflammation; saturated fat at 6.25 g/100g from ghee and cream exceeds the RA threshold and does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, substitute lamb with chicken or fish, request reduced ghee and cream, and retain the turmeric component for its curcumin anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh is a premium Awadhi-Mughlai slow-cooked lamb shank dish originating from the royal kitchens of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India, and served in Indian fine-dining and casual restaurants in Singapore. The dish features elegantly exposed lamb shanks braised in a rich qorma gravy — a slow-cooked, nut-thickened sauce made from cashew paste, cream, ghee, and aromatic whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf. The preparation uses the traditional dum pukht technique, in which the pot is sealed with dough or a tight lid to trap steam and cook the meat at low heat for several hours, resulting in fall-off-the-bone tenderness and deep flavour penetration. The dish is finished with saffron strands dissolved in warm milk, which impart a golden hue and floral aroma, and bone marrow from the shank enriches the gravy with a silky, unctuous texture. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 187.5 kcal, 13.75 g protein, 12.5 g fat (of which 6.25 g is saturated), and 5.0 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in moderate portions. However, the high saturated fat content (6.25 g/100g) and trans fat (0.25 g/100g) raise cardiovascular and hypertension concerns, particularly at the standard 400 g restaurant serving size. The dish is halal-certified and gluten-free. It is best consumed as an occasional meal rather than a dietary staple for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry with gravy and USDA FoodData Central lamb shank (braised), which are the closest approved-source analogues. The input record's per_100g values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific Awadhi lamb shank preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the approved sources for this dish category at the required specificity. 3. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Mutton curry with gravy' and USDA FoodData Central NDB 17082 (lamb shank, braised); the input record's values are consistent with this composite and are retained. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dum Pukht Koh-e-Awadh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The original input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a menu-listing reference only and is not used as a nutrient evidence source. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.25 g/100g is retained from the input record; naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) are present in ruminant meat and dairy at low levels, consistent with this value. 7. Ethnic tags 'Biryani' and 'Naan' from the original input are not applicable to this dish and have been removed from the cuisine classification.
